title: publishing UDP port does not work in rootful container, but does work in rootless

comments.body:
```
		meta l4proto udp udp dport 19132 counter packets 0 bytes 0 dnat to 10.88.0.197:19132
```

Rule seems to be there, but 0 packets hit it.

I'm really familiar with nftables, though, so I'm not sure what the cause of that could be.

Can I ask for more details about your system - are you entirely on nftables, or do you have iptables installed with the nftables-compatible backend? A dump of firewall rules in iptables format would be somewhat more useful, because I (and the other maintainers) are a bit more familiar with that.

> Rule seems to be there, but 0 packets hit it.

This may be because I dumped the rules immediately after creating a new container, since I deleted the old container I was using when I encountered the bug, and created a fresh container to get the `nftables` output for the bug report.

. . . I just tried it again, and it seems the port forwarding is now working. Unfortunately, I don't remember if there was anything I did differently to cause the problem a few nights ago, but it seems to be working now.

Apologies for the spurious bug report. Feel free to close this issue.
I'll go ahead and close, but feel free to re-open if it reoccurs. Thanks!

@mheon I've run into the bug again today. After a reboot, a container that used to be working now is no longer working, so it seems to be an intermittent problem. I did test it a few times first this time, so the packets should have had a chance to hit the relevant rules. Here is a full dump of my `iptables` rules:

Other tables are omitted since they seem to be empty.

0 packets are hitting CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT, which appears to be our problem. I can see 128 packets hit the rule targetting it in PREROUTING but none of them were sent on...
@mccv1r0 Any advice here? The rules here seem correct, but it doesn't seem like packets are getting to the dnat rules.

This: `I just tried it again, and it seems the port forwarding is now working. ` is interesting.  Leads one to believe that rules are setup properly and something else is going on.

What type of traffic are you sending to `udp/19132`?   Is there a test case that doesn't work?

Things to look out for;

- Make sure the MTU is small enough to fit on the `podman0` interface.
- Make sure your tests are using IPv4.  (since you only show IPv4 firewall rules)

I tried using: `$ sudo  podman run --detach --name="udp-test" -p 19132:19132/udp [debug-image]` and things work.

```$ podman version 
Version:            1.8.0
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.12.15
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
$
```

I thought I reproduced the problem using `netcat` only to find out the client was sending using IPv6 (because I didn't use `nc -4 localhost 19132` .  I did see IPv6 packets in the container, but my test app wasn't listening on so things didn't work.   Once I changed to use IPv4, worked as expected.

> What type of traffic are you sending to udp/19132? Is there a test case that doesn't work?

I'm trying to set up a Minecraft Bedrock Edition server. I know the game uses UDP port 19132 to communicate with IPv4 clients, and UDP port 19133 to communicate with IPv6 clients. Using `tcpdump udp port 19132 -vv` on both the host and the container shows that the host *is* receiving packets on UDP port 19132, but the container is not. I believe `tcpdump` should display both IPv4 and IPv6 packets, no?

Networking is not my strong suit unfortunately, so I'm not quite sure what else I could do to test it further. Feel free to let me know what further tests I should run!

> Make sure your tests are using IPv4. (since you only show IPv4 firewall rules)

I am trying to access a server I have set up on my LAN from a different computer in my LAN. The router *should* be assigning only IPv4 addresses on the LAN.

Using `ip6tables` shows that all tables are empty. Is that expected? It would seem that means Podman does not by default support publishing rootful container ports on an IPv6 network?

> Make sure the MTU is small enough to fit on the podman0 interface.

I'm not sure what this means. How would I test this? The case when it was working earlier was with the exact same server and client; I'm not sure if that resolves the question.

Okay, I believe I have found the problem. It was due to the 
```
iifname wan0 ct state {established, related} accept
iifname wan0 drop
```
rules. Since UDP packets don't have a state, the packets were being rejected. Adding a `udp dport {19132, 19133} accept` rule to the forward chain seems to have solved the problem.
